title: Risograph aesthetics for zines, working with restricted colour and halftone
subtitle: How to design for the distinctive look of riso printing even without a riso printer
excerpt: Risograph printing produces a specific visual quality: flat spot colours, visible halftone dots, slightly imprecise registration, and ink that sits on the surface of uncoated paper. These constraints produce a distinctive aesthetic that has become strongly associated with independent zine culture. This article explains what produces the look and how to aim for it deliberately.

glossaryTerms.definition: A stencil duplicator manufactured by Riso Kagaku Corporation, used for short-run printing with soy-based inks. It prints one colour at a time, producing flat spot colours with visible halftone texture and slight misregistration between passes.
A printing method in which each colour is applied as a single, undiluted ink rather than as a mix of process colours. Risograph uses spot colours; standard inkjet printers use CMYK process colour.

A printing technique in which continuous-tone areas are broken into a grid of dots of varying size or spacing to simulate shading. Risograph halftone dots are large enough to be visible to the naked eye, which is a key part of the aesthetic.

content.text: look has four parts: two or three
dots, uncoated paper with visible texture, and slightly imprecise colour registration. Any of these can be referenced in hand-drawn or photocopied zines without a riso printer.

Riso printing typically uses two or three colours per print run because each colour requires a separate ink drum. Zines designed for riso are therefore deliberately limited in palette. The simplest starting point is black plus one

, fluorescent pink, medium blue, and bright orange are common riso ink colours. Where the two colours overlap, they produce a third mixed colour. Designing explicitly for this overlap, rather than avoiding it, is characteristic of the aesthetic.

is referenced by drawing areas of stippling or regular dot grids rather than solid fills. A dense stipple reads as a dark tone; a sparse one reads as light. Keeping the dots at a consistent, visible size produces the printed quality rather than the looser texture of freehand shading. A fine-tipped pen and a light pencil grid to keep the dots regular is the simplest approach.

Riso inks are designed for uncoated, slightly absorbent paper. The ink sits partly on the surface and partly absorbed into the paper, which produces the characteristic slightly chalky, non-glossy colour. Photocopier paper works; cream or tinted uncoated stock looks more deliberate. Avoid coated or glossy paper, it does not reference the riso aesthetic and causes problems if the zine is later taken to a riso printer.

Riso printers have slight misregistration between colour passes, the second colour lands 1-3 mm off from the first. In hand-drawn work this is referenced by deliberately offsetting a second colour element slightly from its partner. A black outline with a coloured shadow offset by 2-3 mm is a simple version. The offset should be consistent across the page and in a single direction.
